1.pericope_label: A Plea for Rescue from Enemies
1.synthesis: Psalm 35 is an extended imprecatory lament, addressing enemies with striking legal and martial imagery while anchoring the psalmist’s case entirely in YHWH’s justice. The opening marshals divine assistance in military language: “Contend, O Lord, with those who contend with me; fight against those who fight against me! Take hold of shield and buckler and rise for my help!” (Ps 35:1–2). The psalmist prays for his persecutors to be like chaff before the wind, with the angel of the Lord driving them away (35:5–6). The injustice is catalogued: without cause they dug a pit; without cause they hid their net for the psalmist (35:7). When his enemies were sick, he wore sackcloth and mourned for them “as one who laments his mother” (35:14); yet at his stumbling they gathered in glee, “gnashing at me with their teeth” (35:16). The critical phrase: “Malicious witnesses rise up; they ask me of things I do not know. They repay me evil for good; my soul is bereft” (35:11–12). The psalm closes with anticipatory praise: “Let those who delight in my righteousness shout for joy and be glad and say evermore, ‘Great is the Lord, who delights in the welfare of his servant!’” (35:27).
1.historical_context: The imprecatory psalms (including 35, 58, 69, 109, 137) have been a persistent pastoral and theological challenge to interpreters. Their curses are not personal vendettas but appeals to the divine judge to execute justice against those who have inverted the moral order—repaying good with evil, offering false testimony, gloating over suffering. The phrase “they hated me without a cause” (35:19) is quoted by Jesus in John 15:25 as describing the world’s hostility to him: “This is to fulfill the word that is written in their Law: ‘They hated me without a cause.’” The psalmist’s mourning for his enemies when they were ill (35:13–14) demonstrates a moral seriousness that makes their subsequent betrayal not merely painful but bewildering: the relationship was one of genuine goodwill, not mere tolerance, making the unprovoked hostility all the more unjust.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 35 divides into three parallel movements, each containing petition, lament of enemies’ behavior, and anticipatory praise: vv. 1–10 (military petition, unjust net-laying, praise for YHWH’s rescue), vv. 11–18 (false witnesses, psalmist’s mourning vs. enemies’ gloating, appeal for rescue), and vv. 19–28 (groundless hatred, malicious winking, final anticipatory praise). This triple structure suggests that the psalm rehearses the same situation from three angles, deepening the injustice each time and correspondingly deepening the confidence in YHWH’s eventual vindication. The closing verse—“my tongue shall tell of your righteousness and of your praise all the day long” (35:28)—promises what the psalmist’s enemies have been attempting to silence: the ongoing testimony of a life shaped by divine justice.
1.theological_application: The imprecatory psalms require communities to wrestle with the moral seriousness of injustice. Psalm 35 is not an expression of personal spite but of legal protest: the psalmist brings his case to the divine court because human courts have failed. The curses are prayers, not actions—the psalmist’s power to harm is surrendered to YHWH, not exercised independently. Communities shaped by this psalm learn to distinguish between private vengeance (forbidden) and the appeal to divine justice (commanded): “Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God, for it is written, ‘Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord’” (Rom 12:19). The psalmist’s mourning over his enemies’ illness (35:13–14) demonstrates that the desire for justice is not the same as the desire for their harm—the psalmist loved them, which is what makes the betrayal so acute.
1.christology: “They hated me without a cause” (35:19) is Christ’s own identification of the world’s hostility toward him (John 15:25): the gratuitous, causeless hatred that met Jesus—who “went about doing good” (Acts 10:38)—is the fulfillment of this psalm’s protest. Malicious witnesses rose against him at his trial (Matt 26:60); they repaid good with evil, demanding his crucifixion after he had healed their sick. The petition that his enemies’ plans be frustrated and his vindication be visible to the congregation (35:27) was answered in the resurrection: “Great is the Lord who delights in the welfare of his servant” became the Easter proclamation, as God vindicated the one against whom false witnesses had spoken.
